coredata.dc:title: Heteroatom co-doping (N, NS, NB) on carbon dots and their antibacterial and antioxidant properties

coredata.dc:description: The development of new nanoparticle-based antibiotics with biocompatible properties is an emerging advance in nanotechnology. This study advocated the development of carbon dots (CDs) doped with nitrogen, nitrogen with sulfur, and nitrogen with boron (N, NS, and NB-CDs). This led to changes in the properties of the CDs, both chemically and biologically. A facile hydrothermal technique was used to synthesize CDs and the formation of CDs was confirmed through various analytical techniques. The CDs had sizes ranging from 3.2–4.8 nm and ζ-potential values of +13 to 27 mV. The doped CDs exhibited moderate changes in fluorescence behaviors depending on the excitation wavelength (λex). The N- and NB-doped CDs were effective at eliminating gram-negative pathogens (E. coli and K. pneumoniae), with minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of 300 µg/mL and 400 µg/mL, respectively. The bactericidal effect of these CDs was attributed to the positive surface charge of the doped CDs and the production of ROS, which caused damage to the bacterial membrane and led to cell death. N- and NS-CDs also showed excellent free radical scavenging activities (<90 %) for DPPH and ABTS. The biocompatibility of doped CDs was examined by a human leukemia monocytic cell line (THP1 cell) and hemolysis test, which found that N, NB-CDs showed over 80 % viable cells at equivalent bactericidal (MBC) concentrations compared with NS-doped CDs. Our findings suggest that doping CDs with various dopants could be a new approach for selectively eliminating bacterial pathogens in hospital and biomedical environments.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Experimental procedures for quantum yield (QY) measurement, time-dependent bacterial eradication, photostability, fluorescence decay (lifetime), and antioxidant assays. Results of hydrodynamic diameter, quantum yield, plate images of bacterial growth, bacterial survival rate, time dependent bacterial eradication, apparent Zeta potential of CDs, protein-leakage, UV-results of DPPH, ABTS, OH radical elimination, and comparison table. This work was supported by EC Project SYMSITE (101058426), in the frame of Horizon 2020. We want to thank Prof. Ehud Banin, Biofilm laboratory, The Mina and Everard Goodman Faculty of Life Sciences, Bar-Ilan University, for providing bacterial strains. This is to certify that the manuscript entitled “Heteroatom co-doping (N, NS, NB) on carbon dots and their effect of antibacterial and antioxidant properties” has been read and approved by all named authors and that there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are not listed. We further confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been approved by all of us. Further, this is to certify that the authors don't have any conflicts of Interest and accept the paper for its possible publication in the Journal.

head.ce:title: Heteroatom co-doping (N, NS, NB) on carbon dots and their antibacterial and antioxidant properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of new nanoparticle-based antibiotics with biocompatible properties is an emerging advance in nanotechnology. This study advocated the development of carbon dots (CDs) doped with nitrogen, nitrogen with sulfur, and nitrogen with boron (N, NS, and NB-CDs). This led to changes in the properties of the CDs, both chemically and biologically. A facile hydrothermal technique was used to synthesize CDs and the formation of CDs was confirmed through various analytical techniques. The CDs had sizes ranging from 3.2–4.8 nm and ζ-potential values of +13 to 27 mV. The doped CDs exhibited moderate changes in fluorescence behaviors depending on the excitation wavelength (λex). The N- and NB-doped CDs were effective at eliminating gram-negative pathogens (E. coli and K. pneumoniae), with minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of 300 µg/mL and 400 µg/mL, respectively. The bactericidal effect of these CDs was attributed to the positive surface charge of the doped CDs and the production of ROS, which caused damage to the bacterial membrane and led to cell death. N- and NS-CDs also showed excellent free radical scavenging activities (<90 %) for DPPH and ABTS. The biocompatibility of doped CDs was examined by a human leukemia monocytic cell line (THP1 cell) and hemolysis test, which found that N, NB-CDs showed over 80 % viable cells at equivalent bactericidal (MBC) concentrations compared with NS-doped CDs. Our findings suggest that doping CDs with various dopants could be a new approach for selectively eliminating bacterial pathogens in hospital and biomedical environments.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogens cause various bacterial infections and millions of deaths annually, significantly impacting the worldwide mortality burden. Bacterial infections in humans cause 60 % of bacterial diseases worldwide and have become the most significant public threat in the world [1]. A dramatic rise in mortality and morbidity associated with traditional therapeutic strategies has increased the demand for an effective antibacterial agent. Nanoparticle-based antimicrobial therapeutics have been approached in biomedical sciences since commercial antibiotics have become ineffective due to an upsurge of drug-resistant pathogen strains [2]. In recent decades, some of the carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) such as graphene, fullerene, nanodiamonds, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon dots (CDs) have been used to inhibit and eliminate bacterial pathogens [3]. Amongst CBNs, carbon dots (CDs) are familiar nanoparticles of carbon-based nanomaterials [4].

Over the past decade, carbon dots (CDs or C-dots) have become zero-dimensional nanomaterials. They exhibit unique fluorescence characteristics since they are smaller (less than 10 nm). In addition, CDs unveil other superior properties such as water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and surface group functionalities, unlike other commercially available semiconductor quantum dots. It has already been reported that CDs have adhesion and adsorption properties to biological surfaces, making them attractive nanomaterials in the biomedical fields such as biosensing, bioimaging, bio-targeting, photodynamic therapy (PDT), and drug delivery [5,6]. Recently, CDs have been highly motivated as theragnostic agents for antioxidants, antibacterial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral agents, opening new applications to the CDs. Several types of carbon dots have been explored for their potential antibacterial properties against various pathogens, including non-metal-doped CDs (Nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous) [7–10], green-synthesized CDs [11], metal-doped CDs [12,13], etc.

The effective antibacterial activity of CDs/CQDs is linked to their surface charge (positive or negative) [14] and ROS (hydroxyl, singlet oxygen) generation, which can disturb cell membrane integrity. Previous studies have confirmed that positively and negatively charged CQDs/CDs can eliminate methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Also, Wang and co-workers examined the effective eradication of N-CQDs on MRSA through DNA damage and ROS production [15]. The intrinsic chemical nature and available functional groups of CDs can be tuned by introducing different atoms into the carbon core of CDs [9,16]. The two main strategies to improve the electronic nature, optical properties, and surface chemical properties of CDs are heteroatom doping and surface functionalization. Among trace elements, nitrogen (N), boron (B), and sulfur (S) can be incorporated into CDs to adjust the generation of n-type or p-type carriers [17]. Doping with N, B, and S elements is frequently used because of their low atomic weight, which makes them safer, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly than metal and semiconductor elements with large atomic weights. Multiatom co-doping strategies have been used to modulate and regulate the physical and chemical properties of CDs, including surface charge (+/-), electronic density, charge transfer, and functional groups (OH, COOH, NH2, CH3) [18]. Introducing active functional groups on the surface of CDs by multi-heteroatom functionalization leads to biocompatibility with less toxicity and improves functional properties such as antibacterial and antioxidant. Recently, CDs have been fabricated with nitrogen-boron and nitrogen-sulfur by incorporating N, B, S, and P as dopants into carbon sources and have been applied in biomedical applications [19,20]. Moreover, heteroatomic CDs with nitrogen dopants have been reported to have more potent bactericidal activity against Escherichia coli (E. coli), S. aureus, and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) than the original nature of CDs [21–24]. So far, only a few studies have reported on heteroatom co-doping with multiatom and its antibacterial and antioxidant activities. However, compared to mono atomically doped CDs, systematic studies on the co-doping of nitrogen with sulfur and boron and their efficiency toward bacterial elimination are still lacking.

Therefore, this study aimed to synthesize heteroatom co-doped CDs (N, NS, and NSB-CDs) and examine whether the doping of nitrogen with sulfur and boron differentiates the antibacterial activity by generating distinct types of ROS, charge, and surface chemistry (specific configuration of heteroatoms). Also, we have emphasized checking the free radical scavenging potential of these different atom-doped CDs. We adopted our previously reported simple hydrothermal carbonization method with slight modifications. Precursors, such as glucosamine, were used as carbon and nitrogen sources, whereas maleic and boric acids were used as sulfur and boron sources, respectively. To study their antibacterial properties, we used gram-negative (E. coli and K. pneumoniae) and gram-positive (S. aureus and S. epidermidis) bacteria as model pathogens. The role of the surface chemistry of the multiatom co-doping effect on the antibacterial mechanism was extensively analyzed and linked to the antibacterial efficiency of the synthesized CDs.

Chemicals: Glucosamine hydrochloride (GA), maleic acid (MA), thiomalic acid (TMA), boric acid (BA), Luria-Bertani (LB) broth, agar, fluorescein 5(6)-isothiocyanate (FITC), propidium iodide (PI), 3-4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), dichloro-dihydro-fluorescein diacetate (DCFH), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), Bradford reagent, and bovine serum albumin (BSA), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, Israel. Activated carbon was purchased from Fisher Scientific.

Bacterial strain and culture: Gram-negative pathogens ( E. coli, ATCC25922 and Klebsiella pneumoniae, ATCC 700603) and Gram-positive pathogens (Staphylococcus aureus, ATCC29213 and Staphylococcus epidermidis, ATCC 12228) were inoculated into LB (Luria-Bertani) media in a culture tube and cultured overnight at 37 °C with agitation at 120 rpm. The density of the overnight cultures was measured at 595 nm (OD595) and adjusted in fresh LB medium for subsequent assays.

The morphology of CDs was analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL-2100, Peabody, MA, USA). The samples were prepared by drop-casting onto a copper-coated grid and drying at 45−50 °C overnight. The optical properties (fluorescence and UV-absorbance) were measured using a Varian Cary Eclipse with a xenon lamp as the excitation source and a Varian Cary 100 Bio Spectrophotometer. The surface functional groups were collected using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Scientific, USA) equipped with a Smart iTR attenuated total reflectance sampler containing a single bounce diamond crystal. The spectrum range was between 500 and 4000 cm−1 with 24 scans (4 cm−1 resolution). The surface charge and hydration particle size charge were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements with a Malvern Zeta sizer Nano-ZS (Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Nexsa spectrometer, England) was acquired to verify the elemental composition and chemical structure of the CDs. The binding energy of the C1s peak at 285 eV was utilized to tune binding energies for all elements. Bacterial surface morphology was assessed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (F-SEM) with an FEI Megallen 400 L microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of doped CDs (N, NS, and NB-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A facile one-step hydrothermal carbonization method was used to synthesize CDs. To synthesize N, NS, and NB-CDs, the respective precursors such as glucosamine, maleic acid, thiomalic acid, and boric acid were used, as indicated in Table S1 (SI). The precursors were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (18.2 MΩ, 25 ± 2 °C). The solution was treated ultrasonically for 15 min and heated in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave tube at 180 °C for 12 h in a hot air oven. The obtained brown product was purified by centrifugation (9000 rpm, 20 min) and the supernatant solution was passed through a micropore film filter (0.22 µm) for thrice to remove larger particles. Then, the CD solutions were further purified in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500, 24 h) against distilled water to remove unreacted particles and obtain pure CDs. The purified N, NS, and NB-CDs were freeze-dried for further characterization, antibacterial, and antioxidant studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MICs) and minimum bactericidal concentrations (MBCs)
ce:section.ce:para:
An equal volume of bacterial suspension (1 × 105 CFU/mL) and CDs at different concentrations (100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 µg mL-1) were mixed in a culture tube and incubated in an orbital shaker (MRC, Israel) at 37 °C for 24 h at 150 rpm. After the incubation, the bacterial suspension was transferred to a 96-well plate and the density of bacterial growth was measured at 595 nm (OD595) using a microplate reader (BioTeck, TM H1/H1M, USA). A bacterial solution without any nanomaterial served as a control. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MICs) can inhibit <90 % growth of each bacterial strain after 24 h, whereas minimum bactericidal concentrations (MBCs) were the lowest concentrations that killed 100 % of bacterial cells. The bacterial survival and antibacterial rates were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.

The lowest concentration of CDs required for complete inhibition of bacterial growth was defined as the MIC compared to the control (non-treated).

The above CDs treated bacterial solutions (20 μL) were diluted with 10 % LB medium. Next, 5 µL of serially diluted bacterial suspension was plated on an LB agar-coated plate to count CFU after incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. Untreated bacteria in the LB medium were used as negative controls. Finally, the number of bacterial colonies on the plate was counted and photographed.
The protocol for time-kill studies of CDs at 0.5, 1, and 2 MIC values is given in Section 2.0 of Supporting Information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hydroxyl radical (●OH) by ESR
ce:section.ce:para: X-band electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was used to measure hydroxyl radicals (●OH), with spin trap 5, 5-dimethyl, 1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). A mixture of DMPO and the sample solution was placed in a standard rectangular Bruker EPR cavity (ER 4119 HS) of Bruker ELEXSYSE500cw X-band EPR spectrometer. The ESR spectra were recorded at room temperature with a microwave frequency of 9.8 GHz, microwave power of 20 mW, sweep width of 100 G (centered at 3518 G), and modulation amplitude of 1 G.

Bacterial solutions were prepared in LB medium (OD 0.2 at 595 nm) and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate the supernatants. The pellet was then resuspended in fresh media with the addition of N, NS, and NB-CDs at the corresponding MIC concentrations and incubated at 37 °C for 6 h at 180 rpm. The unbound CDs were removed by washing three times with PBS, and the zeta potential measurements were conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total intracellular ROS detection by DCFH-DA
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular esterase enzymes can modulate non-fluorescent 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein Diacetate (DCFH-DA) into non-fluorescent 2-(2,7- Dichloro-3,6-dihydroxy-9H-xanthen-9-yl) benzoic acid, which is then oxidized to be 2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF) in the presence of ROS. DCF has a strong emission-centered peak at 525 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. Briefly, the bacterial solution was mixed with 2 mL DCFH-DA (10 μM) in a sterile vial. Subsequently, bacterial cells (2 × 107 cells) were treated with CDs at their respective MIC values and incubated for 6 h at 37 °C and 180 rpm. A negative control (bacteria treated with only DCFH-DA) was added to separate vials. The fluorescence intensity (λex/λem = 488 nm/525 nm) was measured using a Synergy H1 microplate reader. The fluorescence intensity of DCF is directly proportional to the number of reactive oxygen species (ROS).

Protein leakage analysis was performed using Bradford assay. The bacterial cells (1 × 107 CFU/ mL) were treated with N, NS, and NB-CDs at their respective MIC values for 12 and 24 h 37 °C and 180 rpm. After treatment, the suspension was centrifuged (5000 rpm for 10 min) and the supernatant was collected. For the Bradford assay, 0.2 mL of the supernatant was mixed in 0.8 mL of the Bradford reagent and incubated for 10 min in the dark. The color changes were observed by measuring optical density at 595 nm. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was used as a standard protein at concentrations of 5–150 µg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images for bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Each bacterial strain (5 × 106 CFU/mL) was incubated with N, NS, and NB-CDs at 37 °C with 180 rpm for 6 h. Then, the bacterial suspensions were centrifuged (5000 rpm, 10 min) to remove CDs, and the pellet was washed with PBS (pH 7.4) three times. Subsequently, the bacteria were centrifuged and re-dispersed in PBS. Bacterial strains were fixed on a poly-l-lysine cover glass, initially using glutaraldehyde 2.5 % solution in the buffer for 2 h, and then incubated with osmium tetroxide 1 % solution. Subsequently, the samples were successively gradient dehydrated with 30, 50, 70, 80, 90, 95, and 100 % (volume ratio) of ethanol. The samples were dried using a critical point carbon dioxide dryer. Later, the bacterial morphology was finally observed by FE-SEM after spraying gold on cover glasses containing bacteria [25].

The membrane integrity was studied using Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM). Bacterial suspensions of E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and S. epidermidis (1 × 108 CFU mL) were mixed with N, NS, and NB-CD solutions at a 1x MIC value and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h (180 rpm). Untreated bacteria in the DDW were used as controls. The mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min and the pellet was resuspended in 100 µL PBS. After, they were stained with a LIVE/DEAD® BacLight™ Bacterial viability kit (Invitrogen Molecular Probes, Eugene, OR, USA) for 30 min. This kit includes a green fluorescent nucleic acid dye SYTO9, which can stain bacteria with intact cell membranes, while propidium iodine dye can stain damaged membranes/dead bacteria with a red pigment. The kit was prepared freshly by mixing both dyes in a 1:1 ratio and then diluted in PBS at a 1:50 ratio [26]. Once bacteria were stained, they were washed with PBS solution to remove any excess dye and reduce the background signals. The fluorescence images were acquired by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (Leica confocal stellaris, USA) under excitation at 488 and 564 nm.
The photostability of doped CDs was investigated under pH and ionic strength is given in SI-Section 3.0. The lifetime measurement of CDs is provided in SI (Section 4.0)
The potential ability of DPPH, and ABTS, ●OH (Hydroxyl) free radical-scavenging ability of the various doped CDs was evaluated. The detailed experimental protocols are provided in SI (Sections 5–7).

The human leukemia monocytic cell line (THP1 cell) was used to evaluate cytotoxicity by the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay. First, THP1monocytes were cultured in RPMI (Roswell Park Memorial Institute) medium supplemented with l-glutamine (5 %) and 10 % fetal FCS. Then, 1 mL of THP1 cells (1 × 106 cells/well) in RPMI (supplemented with l-glutamine (5 %) and 10 % FCS) was seeded at 37 °C in 5 % CO2. Then, N, NS, and NB-CDs (100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 µg/mL) were incubated with the THP1 cell suspension for 24 h at 37 °C, and the control group was treated with PBS. The medium was discarded, and 10 µL of MTT was added and incubated for 4 h. DMSO (100 µL) was added to dissolve the formazan crystals in the cells. The final product of MTT (blue-violet formazan) was measured at 575 nm using a microplate reader and cell viability (%) was calculated using the following formula (3):

The hemolytic properties of the doped CDs were evaluated using human blood which was obtained from healthy human volunteers with the approval of the institution's ethics committee. Anticoagulated blood (5 mL) was centrifuged (2000 rpm, 5 min) and rinsed multiple times with normal saline solution to remove the plasma and extract erythrocytes. The purified erythrocytes were resuspended in a normal saline solution to produce a 25 mL erythrocyte suspension. Erythrocyte suspensions were mixed with various concentrations of CD solution (100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 g/mL) in PBS. Positive (100 % lysis) and negative (0% lysis) control samples were prepared by combining equal volumes (2 mL) of Triton X-100 and a saline solution. The samples were incubated at 37 °C for 90 and 180 min, and erythrocytes and CDs were resuspended by gently shaking the samples once every 30 min. After the incubation period, the samples were centrifuged (2000 rpm, 5 min) and 100 µL of the supernatant was incubated for 30 min at ambient temperature to allow hemoglobin oxidation. The optical density of oxyhemoglobin in the supernatants was determined spectrophotometrically at 540 nm (Nanodrop One UV–Vis Spectrophotometer). Each sample was analyzed in triplicate and the hemolysis ratio was calculated as follows (Eq. 4)

The values are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD) of triplicate experiments (n = 3). A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) test was performed using GraphPad Prism version 5 for Windows (GraphPad Software, San Diego, California, USA) to determine the statistical significance between doped CDs with a significance level of p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and optical properties of N, NS, and NB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological behavior of as-synthesized CDs is presented in Fig. 1 . TEM and HR-TEM images showed that all the doped CDs had monodisperse and spherical shapes with distinct sizes distributions. The particle size of all CDs was between 3 − 6.4 nm (average size 4.8 ± 0.65 nm), 2.0–5.0 nm (average size 3.1 ± 0.34 nm), and 1.8 − 4.6 nm (average size 3.2 ± 0.62 nm) for the N, NS, and NB-CDs, respectively. The average particle sizes of all CDs were measured by measuring the sizes randomly (100 particles) from the TEM images using ImageJ software. The hydrodynamic distribution of CD particles in liquid solution (0.5 mg/mL) examined using DLS was 205.07 ± 14.93 nm, 41.42 ± 3.58 nm, and 107.55 ± 7.45 nm for N, NS, and NB-CDs (Fig.S2). Different atoms doped with CDs exhibited variations in their sizes and their distributions. The quantum yield of N, NS, and NB doped CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (Fig. S3) as standard in 0.1 M H2SO4, and found to be quantum yield as 10.09 %, 22.32 %, and 4.76 %. The doping of multiatom (NS and NB) drastically affects the quantum yield of CDs compared to single atom doping (N).

A series of experiments were done to evaluate the optical properties of the different CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 2 a) showed a peak around 290 nm, which corresponds to the n→π* transition of the CO bond [27]. In addition, the emission spectrum was assessed at various excitation wavelengths, as presented in Fig. 2d. The results demonstrated that the emission was dependent on the excitation wavelength, with the highest emission observed at 380 nm with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NS-CDs (Fig. 2b) exhibited three peaks at 254 nm, 297 nm, and 370 nm, which may be ascribed to π→π* transition of CC, n→π* transition of the CO, and functionalized surface state, respectively [28–30]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NB-CDs showed peaks at 256 nm and 288 nm related to π→π* transition of CC, and n→π* transition of the CO band. The peak at 333 nm might have originated from the trapping of excited state energy by surface states [31]. Like N-CDs, similar types of excitation-dependent emission behavior were observed for NS-CDs (Fig. 2e), and NB-CDs (Fig. 2f) [32,33]. The aforementioned characteristic is attributed to the different sizes of the CDs and the distribution of diverse surface states emanating from the numerous functionalities that exist on the surface of the as-prepared CDs [34,35]. The aqueous dispersion of all CDs under daylight showed a yellowish color while they showed bright blue emission under UV irradiation as shown in the inset.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical properties of N, NS, and NB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: From the full XPS survey of NS-CDs (Fig. 3a), four typical peaks at 284 eV (C1s), 400 eV (N1s), 532 eV (O1s), and 163 eV (S2p) are detected. The high resolution of C1s (Fig. 3b) XPS spectrum of NS-CDs deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 285.2, 286.2, and 288.9 eV, which are assigned to CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds, respectively. The high resolution of N1s XPS spectra deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.7 eV and 400.9 eV, which are ascribed to CN or CN and CNC bonds, respectively. The convoluted O1s spectrum displays four predominant peaks at 532, 532.4, 533.4, and 533.9 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, OCOH, and OH, respectively. The spectrum of S2p is deconvoluted into three peaks at 163.2 and 164.3, which are attributed to S2p 3/2, S2p ½, and CS covalent bonds [38]. The elemental analysis of NS-CDs contains carbon (81.47 %), oxygen (16.85 %), nitrogen (0.99 %), and sulfur (0.68 %) (Table 1). The full XPS spectra result confirmed the presence of sulfur and nitrogen in the NS-CDs.

From the full XPS survey of NB-CDs (Fig. 3a), four typical peaks at 284 eV (C1s), 400 eV (N1s), 532 eV (O1s), and 163 eV (B1s) are detected. The high-resolution C1s (Fig. 3b) spectrum was deconvoluted into five peaks, corresponding to C-B (284.3 eV), CC (284.9), CN (285.5 eV), CO (286.4 eV), and CO (288.8 eV), respectively. As shown in the high-resolution N1s spectra, nitrogen exists in three different states at 399.9 eV (CN), 401.6 eV (CNC), and 406.8 eV (NO) [39]. The convoluted O1s spectrum displays two major peaks at 532 eV and 533 eV, which are due to the presence of CO/ BO and CO species, respectively. In addition, the B1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 192.4 eV and 193.3 eV, ascribed to CBN and BO bonds, respectively [38]. The elemental analysis of NS-CDs contains carbon (63.06 %), oxygen (25.94 %), nitrogen (3.9 %), and boron (7.1 %). The results suggest the strong incorporation of boron and nitrogen heteroatoms in the NB-CDs.

The FT-IR confirms the existence of different functional groups on the surface of CDs. The FT−IR spectra of N-CDs, NS-CDs, and NB-CDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The N−CDs spectrum displays a broad band around 3100 cm−1, which is attributed to the amine stretching vibration and a sharp narrow peak at 1695 cm−1 attributed to the carbonyl stretching vibration. The peaks at 1200−1300 cm−1 are assigned to the CC stretching vibration and the peaks at 1000−1100 cm−1 correspond to the CC stretching vibration. The sulfur and boron-doped CDs show minor spectra changes compared to N−CDs. The common peaks at 3000−3100, 1700−1710, 1300−1390, and 1100−1200 (cm−1) are attributed to NH, −CO, NCO, CN, and CO stretching vibrations in the S and B doped CDs. Moreover, the specific peaks at 1400 and 1046 cm−1 are due to the SO and CS stretching vibration of NS-CDs. The peaks at 1190 and 1099 cm−1 are attributed to the CB and BO stretching vibration of NB−CDs. The appearance of the specific peaks confirms that sulfur and boron are doped in N−CDs. The major peaks and their functional groups are provided in Table 2
[40–42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of N, NS, and NSB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability was examined by different pH levels and ionic strength in NaCl aqueous solution. The stabilization CDs at different pH levels are shown in Fig. S4(a). The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was stable under pH 3–9 and a minor reduction was observed at extreme acidic (pH = 1) and basic (pH = 13) pH conditions. However, NS and NB-CDs were noticed with increased PL intensity with increasing pH = 1−11. Fig. S4 (b) represents the impact of ionic strength on the PL of CDs. Ionic concentrations at 0.5−1.0 M solution have a significant effect on the PL intensity of all CDs. The fluorescence decay (lifetime) was generated from iterative deconvolution of the decay with the instrument response function (IRF), as is given in Fig. S4c and Table S2. The observed lifetimes are τ1 = 0.34 ns, τ2 = 1.79 ns, and τ3 = 6.52 ns for N-CDs, τ1 = 0.41 ns, τ2 = 1.32 ns, and τ3 = 5.17 ns for NS-CDs, and τ1 = 0.44 ns, τ2 = 1.97 ns, and τ3 = 7.58 ns for NB-CDs, respectively. The average lifetime of N, NS, and NB-CDs was 1.85, 1.23, and 1.87 ns, respectively. The observed lifetime of all doped CDs is in the nanosecond range and implies that the prepared CDs are more suitable for optoelectronic and biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The antibacterial activity of N, NS, and NB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of synthesized N, NS, and NB-CDs was primarily evaluated using the MIC and CFU methods against E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and S. epidermidis. Each MIC value was determined using different concentrations of CDs and it was considered to be the elimination of bacteria at around 90 %. The MIC of N, NS, and NB-CDs to E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and S. epidermidis were determined at different concentrations and summarized in Table 1. The MIC of N and NB-CDs was similar (300 µg/mL) to that of E. coli, while that of NS-CDs was higher (500 µg/mL) than that of N and NB-CDs. In the case of K. pneumoniae, the MIC was similar for all types of doped CDs (N, NS, and NB). Also, both gram-positive pathogens S. aureus and S. epidermidis required a MIC of 500 µg/mL for NS, NB-CDs, and N-CDs (500 µg/mL) for S. epidermidis. In addition, N-CDs have attained a MIC of 400 µg/mL for S. aureus. Further, we evaluated the bacterial survival rate and antibacterial rate (%) using their optical density (OD595). Fig. S8 shows the survival rate of bacteria after 24 h incubation with the treatment of N, NS, and NB-CDs. It describes that NCDs and NB-CDs treated E. coli had a lower survival rate at a concentration of 300 µg/mL, whereas 400 µg/mL of N, NS, and NB-CDs to K. pneumoniae. Notably, NS-CDs exhibited differently in eliminating E. coli and K. pneumoniae (both gram-negative). The antibacterial rate of all doped CDs was about 90 % at a concentration of 400−500 µg/mL for all bacterial pathogens (Fig. 5 (a,c,e,g)). The antibacterial rate was progressively increased in E. coli with 300 µg/mL of N and NB-CDs. Further, we performed the CFU method to visualize the viable bacterial colonies after treatment with N, NS, and NB-CDs (Fig. 5 (b,d,f,h)). The agar plates without any colonies at a certain concentration are considered MBC for all CDs. The MBC values for all doped CDs are displayed in Table 3 . Fig. S5 shows a complete elimination of gram-negative pathogens (E. coli and K. pneumoniae) by N-CDs at a concentration of 400 µg/mL, whereas NB-CDs were eradicated at 300 (E. coli) and 400 µg/mL (K. pneumoniae) (Fig. S7). However, NS-CDs required 500 and 400 µg/mL to eliminate E. coli and K. pneumoniae, respectively (Fig. S6). In the case of gram-positive pathogens, more than 500 µg/mL was required for all types of CDs. Moreover, we examined the bacterial growth treated with all types of CDs with ½, 1, and 2 MIC values over time (1−48 h). The N, NS, and NB-CDs suppressed the growth of all bacteria until 12 h incubation with ½ MIC value and increased the bacterial population after 12 h of incubation (Fig. S9). Moreover, E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and S. epidermidis were eliminated with a 1 MIC value after 24 h of incubation by N, NS, and NB-CDs. However, the NS-CDs had no effect on S. epidermidis at all MIC values. Also, all doped CDs prevented the growth of all bacterial cells at 2 MIC values until 48 h incubation. This complete eradication of bacterial pathogens at 2 MIC value is due to higher concentrations of CDs and their toxicity on bacterial pathogens. The comparison table of the antibacterial activity of previous CDs and this study is presented in Table S4. Compared to previous literature, the doped CDs selectively eliminated gram-negative pathogens (E. coli and K. pneumoniae).

Previous research revealed that the most effective antibacterial function is related to the formation of ROS [43], surface charge (ζ-potential) [44], and their electrostatic interaction. A high ROS level can upsurge oxidative damage to the cell membrane, genomic DNA, proteins, and subsequently cell death.

Zeta potential: To confirm the surface charge, we examined the Zeta potential of doped CDs with bacterial samples (Fig. S10 and Table 4 ). The ζ-potential results (Table 4) show that the control bacteria are negatively charged, and the doped CDs are positively charged (Fig. S10). Upon interaction of N, NS, and NB-CDs with E. coli and K. pneumoniae, the ζ-potential of bacterial cells significantly changed and reduced their surface charge into the positive direction (E. coli: from −46.9 to −38.2 mV (N-CDs), −35.4 mV (NB-CDs), −33.9 mV (NB-CDs); K. pneumoniae: from −30.4 to −29.9 mV (N-CDs), −26.9 mV (NB-CDs), −24.2 mV (NB-CDs)). In the case of S. aureus, their surface was reduced from −34 to −24.8 mV (N-CDs), −23.9 mV (NS-CDs), and −23.7 mV (NB-CDs). However, NS-CDs and NB-CDs had no significant changes in the surface charge of S. epidermidis. The results confirm that the surface charge (Zeta potential) was anticipated to be more effective in the elimination of gram-negative (E. coli and K. pneumoniae), and gram-positive (S. aureus). Fig. S10 shows the Zeta potential of N, NS, and NB-CDs. The zeta potential of N-CDs was higher than that of S and B-doped CDs. The N-CDs had a surface charge of around 26.9 mV, whereas the NS and NB-CDs were only 16.6 and 13.9, respectively. Notably, though NB-CDs have less zeta potential, they show superior antibacterial properties like N-CDs. Therefore, the surface charge of the doped CDs also influences the antibacterial by generating strong constant electricity with the negatively charged bacterial cell, resulting in increased membrane permeability, leading to bacterial membrane damage and cell death [45].

ROS measurement by ESR: To evaluate the capacity to produce free radicals of different CDs (N-CDs, NS-CDs, and NB-CDs), we studied electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy with DMPO as a trapping agent. Two complementary methods were used: simple spin-trapping (DMPO) and spin-trapping after scavenging with DMSO (DMSO + DMPO). A hyperfine splitting signal was noticed when DMPO was mixed with doped CDs. The strong EPR signal with a 1:2:2:1 pattern (Fig. 6 a) is characteristic of the DMPO–OH adduct, suggesting that CDs can produce OH● [46]. From the ESR spectra, a quartet of DMPO-OH was measured with N, NS, and NB-CD samples.

As shown in Fig. 6b, when DMSO was added, the DMPO-OH signal was reduced in both N-CDs and NS-CDs. A 6-line signal attributed to the DMSO-derived adduct (DMPO-CH● 3) was noticed with N-CDs with a higher concentration of DMPO (253 mM) (Fig. 6b). However, NS-CDs and NB-CDs had no obvious signal changes for the DMSO-derived adduct. The scavenging reactions of dimethyl sulfoxide ((CH3)2 SO) are described by Eqs. (5&6) [47]:
(5) (CH3)2SO + HO● → CH3 SO (OH) + CH● 3
(6) CH3 SO (OH) + CH● 3 + DMPO → DMPO − CH● 3 (CH3)2 SO + HO●
The above findings suggest that CDs can generate ROS, which is a crucial factor in eliminating bacteria.

Intracellular ROS detection by DCFH-A: The intracellular ROS generation by the interacting system of doped CDs with bacterial cells was examined using 2′,7′-dichlor-dihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2-DCFDA) as a fluorescence staining probe. As shown in Fig. 6c-f, the control samples of all bacterial cells had less fluorescence (slightly dead cells) at 6 h of incubation. However, N and NB-CDs showed increased fluorescence intensity compared to NS-CDs and control bacteria. Also, N and NB-CDs showed more intracellular oxidative damage in E. coli and K. pneumoniae than gram-positive pathogens (S. aureus and S. epidermidis). The prevailing hypothesis is that the antibacterial mechanism of CDs produces intracellular ROS upon interaction with bacterial cells, leading to oxidative damage to cell membranes and proteins and subsequently resulting in cell death.

Morphological analysis by FE-SEM and CLSM: Further, the bacterial cell surface membrane damage was studied through FE-SEM (Fig. 7 ). The changes on the bacterial surface were observed after treating the bacterial cells with all kinds of doped CDs at their respective MIC values for 6 h. Compared to control bacteria with smooth surfaces, CDs (N, NS, and NB-CDs) treated bacteria reflected clear collapsed, wrinkled, and distorted (surface with holes) surfaces, indicating a remarkable change on the surface of E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. epidermidis, while there was fewer membrane damage on S. aureus. Especially, bacterial cells treated with N and NB-CDs had significant damage on the cell surface. The N and NB-CDs have bound to the cell membrane without any obvious damage, indicating less pronounced effects on S. aureus. Morphological analysis of the bacterial membrane surface through FE-SEM confirms a remarkable change in the bacterial cell membrane.

Confocal laser scanning electron microscopy (CLSM): Also, the cell membrane damage of bacteria by N, NS, and NB-CDs was ascertained by CLSM using a staining experiment with SYTO9 and PI (Fig. 8 ). SYTO9 can stain both live and dead cells, while PI can only penetrate and stain dead cells through damaged membranes, causing them to bind with nucleic acids. When bacterial cells were treated with both SYTO9 (membrane permeable) and PI (membrane impermeable), control samples of gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and K. pneumoniae) showed bright green fluorescence with the absence of red fluorescence, implying more viable cells and no damage to the cell membrane. While N and NB-treated CDs showed fewer bacterial cells with less green and red fluorescence. However, NS-CDs treated samples showed bright green (viable cells) fluorescence with lesser red fluorescence. The population of N and NB-CDs treated bacterial cells was less with more membrane-damaged cells, indicating the growth of bacterial cells was suppressed by N and NB-CDs at 6 h of incubation, and the rest of the bacterial cells were stained with PI. As revealed by FE-SEM and CLSM, CDs could interact with surface species, such as lipopolysaccharide or lipoteichoic acid on the bacterial membrane via noncovalent interactions, such as electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, or van der Waals attraction. The CDs could then accumulate within the lipid bilayer via hydrophobic interaction, resulting in membrane permeabilization. This causes cytoplasmic content to be released, which ultimately causes cell death.

Intracellular protein leakage: Proteins play a crucial role in the cellular process and their excretion during intracellular damage is vital in cellular development. Herein, we examined the protein leakage resulting from intracellular damage with the Bradford assay (Fig. S11). The released proteins increased with increasing incubation time, resulting in cellular protein damage of doped CDs. Elimination of protein levels in the supernatant at 12 h was not more significant (p < 0.05) at 12 h of incubation. However, more proteins were released from E. coli (53.6 and 54 µg/mL) S. aureus (77 and 71.13 µg/mL), S. epidermidis (34 and 27.6 µg/mL) after exposing N and NS-CDs for 24 h. However, in CDs treated with K. pneumoniae, the protein level was insignificant compared with control bacteria, indicating non-intracellular protein damage. These results demonstrate the permeabilization of nanoparticles into bacterial cells and their simultaneous actions on protein content. The differences in the protein leakage from gram-positive and gram-negative bacterial cells damaged by N, NS, and NB-CDs result in either destroying the protein or arresting protein synthesis, leading to cell death.

The overall bacterial mechanism of bacterial death elaborated and found that the surface charge of different CDs and their surface attachment with the bacterial cell membrane (electrostatic interactions) helps to internalize CDs inside bacterial cells via surface membrane damage. Further, these CDs generate ROS inside the cell and cause oxidative damage to cellular organelles (DNA, proteins) which are crucial for cellular metabolism. The proposed mechanism of cell death is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant potential of N, NS, and NB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We further examined the antioxidant potential of doped CDs by scavenging DPPH and ABTS radicals. As depicted in Fig. 9 (a), the percentage of DPPH radical reduction was observed with increased concentrations of CDs (100–500 µg/mL) (Fig. S12), and the percentage of DPPH inhibition activity increased from 27 to 80 % (N-CDs), 45−83 % (NS-CDs), and 14−42 % (NB-CDs). In comparingly, N and NS-CDs exhibited significant DPPH radical inhibition when compared with NB-CDs and standard ascorbic acid (up to 88 % at 500 µg/mL). Similarly, the reduction in the color of ABTS•+ was noticed with the increasing concentration of doped CDs (Fig. S13), accompanied by the ABTS•+ elimination being increased from 23 to 70 % (N-CDs), 40–85 % (NS-CDs), and 15–48 % (NB-CDs) (Fig. 9b). Though, as shown in Fig. 9c, doped CDs did not show any improvement in eliminating hydroxyl radicals except N-CDs with an increasing concentration (<90 % at 500 μg/mL). The UV-absorption spectra of OH radical solution showed a decreased absorption pattern for N-CDs and NS-CDs with increasing CDs concentrations (Fig. S14). Notably, NB-CDs had an inability in all types of radical elimination, compared with N and NS-CDs. It has been reported that electron-donating doped sites and sp2 carbon domains are important for eliminating free radicals through adduct development and spin delocalization in the carbon backbone. The doping of elements can help with scavenging free radicals by donating electrons and forming adducts. For a better insight into the interaction, the radical scavenging mechanism of CDs is described. It is widely hypothesized that both the surface oxygen and nitrogen functionalities play a significant role in the scavenging of ROS [48–52]. The reason for this condition can be ascribed to the valence states and covalent radius. The presence of electron-donating doped sites and sp2 carbon domains has been observed to correlate with the principal radical scavenging sites. These sites play a crucial role in the elimination of free radicals through adduct formation and spin delocalization along the conjugated carbon backbone [48,53]. Dopants may aid in radical scavenging by transferring electrons to radicals, followed by the transfer of a proton or the production of an adduct [54,55]. The scavenging capabilities of CDs can potentially be attributed to the hydrogen-donor characteristics exhibited by the functional groups present at the edges of the carbon network. These functional groups, namely hydroxyl (OH), carboxyl (COOH), thiol (SH), and amino (NH2), act along with the delocalization and stabilization of free electrons within the sp2-hybridized carbon network. Herein, the excellent radical scavenging effect is probably due to the ability of CDs to donate H+ ions based on the existence of functional groups (COOH, OH/NH2) on N and NS-CDs to form a DPPH2 stable complex [52]. Since the N and NS-doped CDs had both COOH and NH2 (derived from maleic, thiomalic acid, and glucosamine), these doped CDs exhibited excellent performance in the elimination of radicals compared to NB-CDs (not derived acid functional groups from boric acid). The IC50 values were found to be 265 μg/mL (N-CDs) and 145 μg/mL (NS-CDs), while 220 μg/mL (N-CDs), 120 μg/mL (NS-CDs) for inhibition of ABTS•+ and DPPH inhibition. These variations and excellent antioxidant activity of these doped CDs may be due to the abundance of different functional groups on the surface participating in the free radical elimination to produce more stable complexes in DPPH and ABTS assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of doped CDs (N, NS, and NB)
ce:section.ce:para: As it is hypothesized that CDs can be used in bioimaging, target drug delivery, gene therapy, anticancer, and antimicrobial applications, it is crucial to evaluate their biosafety and biocompatibility. The biocompatibility of N, NS, and NB-CDs was assessed by MTT assay using macrophage cell lines (Fig. 10 a). It showed clearly that N-doped CDs showed no toxicity at their bactericidal concentrations (300 µg/mL, E. coli) and had toxicity above the MIC value. In addition, NS-doped CDs had more toxicity with an increase in concentrations up to 400−500 µg/mL compared to N-CDs. NB-CDs showed a non-toxicity between 300 and 400 µg/mL (MIC for E. coli and K. pneumoniae) and negligible toxicity at 500 µg/mL (MIC for S. aureus and S. epidermidis) with 80 % cell survival. Thus, NB-CDs can be used as a promising nano agent for eliminating microbial pathogens at lower concentrations with good biocompatibility.

To determine the biocompatibility of CDs, a hemolysis assay was conducted to examine whether the fluorescent materials are biocompatible when in contact with blood. Fig. 10(b) Shows the hemolysis (%) rate of N, NS, and NB-CDs at different concentrations. When the concentration of all CDs reached 500 µg/mL, the hemolysis rate also increased by 6–15 %. The N, NS, and NB-CDs had significant hemolysis rates of about 8.8 %, 14.9 %, and 9.21 % at 500 µg/mL. However, the hemolysis rate of N and NB-CDs was still lower than 5 % (300−400 µg/mL) when compared to NS-CDs, which was about 11.4 % (300−400 µg/mL). The images in Fig. 10(c) show the clear supernatant up to 300 µg/mL for N and NB-CDs, while NS-CDs show a slight red color due to the lysis of red blood cells. As per the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), hemolysis below 5 % can be considered biocompatible [56].

Three kinds of CDs (N, NS, NB doped) were prepared using a facile hydrothermal process. The NS-CDs showed high PL and QY because of the N, S co-doping effect. N and NB co-doped CDs had lower fluorescence intensity and high antibacterial activity. The mechanism of the antibacterial activity between the doped CDs was elucidated. All doped CDs showed a significant antimicrobial potency and N, NB-doped CDs exhibited potent antibacterial activity against gram-negative pathogens (E. coli and K. pneumoniae) larger than NS-CDs. The synergetic mechanism of antibacterial is related to the adhesion of bacterial cells with positively charged CDs, wrapping, destruction of the cell membrane, and ROS damage. Also, N and NS-CDs showed outstanding action in the elimination of DPPH and ABTS free radicals, as well as hydroxyl radicals by N-CDs. The cytotoxic behavior of CDs is affected by doping strategies with N, S, and B atoms. Among them, NB-CDs exhibit superior antibacterial activities with good biocompatibility at their bactericidal concentration than other doped CDs (N and NS-CDs). Hence, doping of CDs along with nitrogen and nitrogen with sulfur, boron provide developmental opportunities to improve the antibacterial efficiency and other biomedical applications of carbon dots.

Arumugam Saravanan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Poushali Das: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Moorthy Maruthapandi: Formal analysis, Validation. Saurav Aryal: Formal analysis. Shulamit Michaeli: Formal analysis. Yitzhak Mastai: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. John H.T. Luong: Writing – review & editing. Aharon Gedanken: Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

Results of hydrodynamic diameter, quantum yield, plate images of bacterial growth, bacterial survival rate, time dependent bacterial eradication, apparent Zeta potential of CDs, protein-leakage, UV-results of DPPH, ABTS, OH radical elimination, and comparison table.
